topic: A person's invention is already patented by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stood by the display case, his knuckles white where he gripped the edge of the velvet rope. The sleek, self-regulating atmospheric condenser he’d spent three years perfecting gleamed under the harsh gallery lights. He watched as a well-dressed investor, Mr. Sterling, circled the device, nodding approvingly. Then, Sterling stopped, his gaze flicking to the small, official plaque beside the condenser. Elias’s stomach plummeted. The inscription clearly stated: "Patent Pending: Sterling Innovations." A slow, hot flush crept up Elias’s neck, staining his cheeks crimson. He mumbled something about needing to consult with legal counsel, his voice sounding thin and reedy in the hushed room. Every glance from the surrounding patrons felt like a spotlight, magnifying the sheer inadequacy of his achievement.

[story 2]

“Remarkable work, Mr. Vance,” the judge boomed, his voice echoing off the marble walls. Elias managed a stiff bow, his hands trembling slightly as he adjusted his tie. He’d poured his soul into the kinetic energy harvester, a device that promised to revolutionize urban power grids. But as the legal representative approached, a crisp document was slid across the table. The title, printed in bold, was unmistakable: "Utility Patent No. 884-B: Sterling Dynamics." Elias felt a sudden, intense heat bloom across his face, making him want to sink into the floor. He cleared his throat repeatedly, trying to force a casual demeanor, but his hands kept fiddling with the cuff of his jacket, a clear sign of internal turmoil. He just wanted to disappear.

[story 3]

I leaned against the wall, trying to appear nonchalant while watching the presentation. My prototype—the sonic filtration unit—was supposed to be the future. I’d worked sleepless nights, fueled by lukewarm coffee and sheer stubbornness. When the keynote speaker mentioned the existing intellectual property surrounding my design, a cold dread washed over me, settling deep in my chest. I felt my skin prickle, an uncomfortable clamminess spreading across my arms. When the panel moderator asked for my thoughts, my mouth went dry. I swallowed hard, the sound loud in the sudden silence, and managed only a weak, "It’s… a complex area." I avoided looking at anyone, focusing instead on the pattern of the expensive carpet beneath my feet, wishing the ground would swallow me whole.
